author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

originalBook: In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.
title: Chapter 104

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 104.—236.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "David also said, ‘The oil of the sinner shall not anoint my head.’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Who is it, therefore, that he calls a sinner?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it I who suffer your violence, or you who persecute the innocent?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: As representing the body of Christ, which is the Church of the living God, the pillar and mainstay of the truth, dispersed throughout the world, on account of the gospel which was preached, according to the words of the apostle, "to every creature which is under heaven:" as representing the whole world, of which David, whose words you cannot understand, has said, "The world also is stablished, that it cannot be moved;" whereas you contend that it not only has been moved, but has been utterly destroyed: as representing this, I answer, I do not persecute the innocent.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But David said, "The oil of the sinner," not of the traditor; not of him who offers incense, not of the persecutor, but "of the sinner."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What then will you make of your interpretation?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: See first whether you are not yourself a sinner.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It is nothing to the point if you should say, I am not a traditor, I am not an offerer of incense, I am not a persecutor.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I myself, by the grace of God, am none of these, nor is the world, which cannot be moved.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But say, if you dare, I am not a sinner.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For David says, "The oil of the sinner."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For so long as any sin, however light, be found in you, what ground have you for maintaining that you are not concerned in the expression that is used, "The oil of the sinner"?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For I would ask whether you use the Lord’s prayer in your devotions?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if you do not use that prayer, which our Lord taught His disciples for their use, where have you learned another, proportioned to your merits, as exceeding the merits of the apostles?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But if you pray, as our great Master deigned to teach us, how do you say, "Forgive us our trespasses, as we forgive them that trespass against us?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For in this petition we are not referring to those sins which have already been forgiven us in baptism.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Therefore these words in the prayer either exclude you from being a petitioner to God, or else they make it manifest that you too are a sinner.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Let those then come and kiss your head who have been baptized by you, whose heads have perished through your oil.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But see to yourself, both what you are and what you think about yourself.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it really true that Optatus, whom pagans, Jews, Christians, men of our party, men of your party, all proclaim throughout the whole of Africa to have been a thief, a traitor, an oppressor, a contriver of schism; not a friend, not a client, but a tool of him whom one of your party declared to have been his count, companion, and god,—is it true that he was not a sinner in any conceivable interpretation of the term?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What then will they do whose heads were anointed by one guilty of a capital offense?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do not those very men kiss your heads, on whose heads you pass so serious a judgment by this interpretation which you place upon the passage?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Truly I would bid you bring them forth, and admonish them to heal themselves.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or is it rather your heads which should be healed, who run so grievously astray?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What then, you will ask, did David really say: Why do you ask me: rather ask himself.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: He answers you in the verse above: "The righteous shall smite me in kindness, and shall reprove me; but let not the oil of the sinner anoint my head."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: What could be plainer? what more manifest?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: I had rather, he says, be healed by a rebuke administered in kindness, than be deceived and led astray by smooth flattery, coming on me as an ointment on my head.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The self-same sentiment is found elsewhere in Scripture under other words: "Better are the wounds of a friend than the proffered kisses of an enemy."
